content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Good morning / Good day'? {{c1::Buongiorno}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good morning / Good day' en Italian? Buongiorno
content.usage: Term: Buongiorno | Meaning: Good morning / Good day

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Good evening'? {{c1::Buonasera}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good evening' en Italian? Buonasera
content.usage: Term: Buonasera | Meaning: Good evening

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Hello / Goodbye - Informal'? {{c1::Ciao}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hello / Goodbye - Informal' en Italian? Ciao
content.usage: Term: Ciao | Meaning: Hello / Goodbye - Informal

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'How are you? - Formal'? {{c1::Come sta?}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'How are you? - Formal' en Italian? Come sta?
content.usage: Term: Come sta? | Meaning: How are you? - Formal

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Goodbye - Formal'? {{c1::Arrivederci}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Goodbye - Formal' en Italian? Arrivederci
content.usage: Term: Arrivederci | Meaning: Goodbye - Formal

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'See you soon'? {{c1::A presto}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you soon' en Italian? A presto
content.usage: Term: A presto | Meaning: See you soon

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Good night'? {{c1::Buonanotte}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good night' en Italian? Buonanotte
content.usage: Term: Buonanotte | Meaning: Good night

content.text: In Italian, 'ciao' is an informal greeting that means both {{c1::hello / hi}} and {{c2::bye / goodbye}}.
content.explanation: 'Ciao' is the most popular informal greeting in Italian. It should not be used in formal contexts.
content.spanish: En italiano, 'ciao' es un saludo informal que significa tanto 'hola' como 'adiós'.
content.usage: Term: Ciao | Dialogue: - Ciao Marco! - Ciao Sofia, come va?

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Good morning / Good day' formally? {{c1::Buongiorno}}
content.explanation: Used from morning until early afternoon as a polite greeting.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenos días / Buen día' formalmente en italiano? Buongiorno.
content.usage: Term: Buongiorno | Example: Buongiorno, Signor Rossi. (Good morning, Mr. Rossi.)

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Good night' (when going to bed)? {{c1::Buonanotte}}
content.explanation: Used specifically when someone is going to sleep or parting late at night.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas noches (al acostarse)' en italiano? Buonanotte.
content.usage: Term: Buonanotte | Example: Buonanotte, sogni d'oro. (Good night, sweet dreams.)

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Goodbye' formally? {{c1::Arrivederci}}
content.explanation: Literally 'until we see each other again'. Used in shops, offices, or with strangers.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Adiós' formalmente en italiano? Arrivederci.
content.usage: Term: Arrivederci | Example: Grazie, arrivederci! (Thank you, goodbye!)

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Please'? {{c1::Per favore}} or {{c1::Per piacere}}.
content.explanation: Both phrases are extremely common and polite.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Por favor' en italiano? Per favore / Per piacere.
content.usage: Examples: Un caffè, per favore. (A coffee, please.) | Mi aiuti, per piacere. (Help me, please.)

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Thank you very much'? {{c1::Grazie mille}}
content.explanation: Literally 'a thousand thanks'. The standard way to show strong appreciation.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Muchas gracias' en italiano? Grazie mille.
content.usage: Term: Grazie mille | Example: Grazie mille per l'aiuto. (Thank you very much for the help.)

content.text: In Italian, the standard response to 'Grazie' is '{{c1::Prego}}'.
content.explanation: 'Prego' means 'You're welcome'. It is also used by waiters or hosts to say 'Please (after you)' or 'How can I help you?'.
content.spanish: En italiano, la respuesta estándar a 'Grazie' es 'Prego' (De nada).
content.usage: Dialogue: - Grazie! - Prego.

content.text: In Italian, the informal way to ask someone their name is '{{c1::Come ti chiami?}}' and the response is '{{c2::Mi chiamo}} [Name]'.
content.explanation: Uses the reflexive verb 'chiamarsi' (to call oneself). Literally: 'How do you call yourself?'.
content.spanish: En italiano, la pregunta informal para preguntar el nombre es 'Come ti chiami?' y la respuesta es 'Mi chiamo...'.
content.usage: Dialogue: - Come ti chiami? - Mi chiamo Jesus.

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Nice to meet you' when meeting someone for the first time? {{c1::Piacere}} or {{c1::Piacere di conoscerti}}.
content.explanation: 'Piacere' alone is very common. 'Piacere di conoscerti' is informal, and 'Piacere di conoscerla' is formal.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Mucho gusto / Encantado' en italiano? Piacere / Piacere di conoscerti.
content.usage: Dialogue: - Mi chiamo Sofia. - Piacere, io sono Marco.

content.text: In Italian, how do you ask 'How are you?' informally? {{c1::Come stai?}} or {{c1::Come va?}}.
content.explanation: 'Come stai?' is for peers/friends. 'Come va?' is generic (How's it going?). The formal version is 'Come sta?'.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo estás?' informalmente en italiano? Come stai? / Come va?.
content.usage: Dialogue: - Ciao, come stai? - Bene, grazie. E tu?

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Have a nice day' upon parting? {{c1::Buona giornata}}
content.explanation: Similar to French 'Bonne journée', used when leaving during the day.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Que tenga un buen día' al despedirse en italiano? Buona giornata.
content.usage: Term: Buona giornata | Example: Grazie, buona giornata! (Thank you, have a nice day!)

content.text: In Italian, a very common and friendly way to say 'See you / See you later' is '{{c1::Ci vediamo}}'.
content.explanation: Literally means 'we see each other'. Used daily among friends and coworkers.
content.spanish: En italiano, una forma muy común y amistosa de decir 'Nos vemos' es 'Ci vediamo'.
content.usage: Term: Ci vediamo | Example: Ciao ragazzi, ci vediamo domani! (Bye guys, see you tomorrow!)

content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'Good day' as a wish when parting? {{c1::Buona giornata}}
content.explanation: Used when leaving during the day. Do not confuse with 'Buongiorno' which is used when arriving.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Que tenga un buen día' al despedirse en italiano? Buona giornata.
content.usage: Term: Buona giornata | Dialogue: - Arrivederci! - Buona giornata!

content.scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'I am'? {{c1::Io sono}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I am' en Italian? Io sono
content.usage: Term: Io sono | Meaning: I am

content.scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'You are'? {{c1::Tu sei}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are' en Italian? Tu sei
content.usage: Term: Tu sei | Meaning: You are

content.scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'He/She is'? {{c1::Lui/Lei è}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'He/She is' en Italian? Lui/Lei è
content.usage: Term: Lui/Lei è | Meaning: He/She is

content.scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'We are'? {{c1::Noi siamo}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'We are' en Italian? Noi siamo
content.usage: Term: Noi siamo | Meaning: We are

content.scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'You are'? {{c1::Voi siete}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are' en Italian? Voi siete
content.usage: Term: Voi siete | Meaning: You are

content.scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Italian, how do you say 'They are'? {{c1::Loro sono}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'They are' en Italian? Loro sono
content.usage: Term: Loro sono | Meaning: They are

content.text: In Italian, the subject pronouns for 'I' and 'you (singular informal)' are {{c1::io}} and {{c2::tu}}.
content.explanation: Italian pronouns are often omitted in sentences because the verb conjugation clearly indicates the subject.
content.spanish: En italiano, los pronombres de sujeto para 'yo' y 'tú' son 'io' y 'tu'.
content.usage: Examples: Io sono stanco. (I am tired.) | Tu sei pronto? (Are you ready?)

content.text: In Italian, the subject pronouns for 'he' and 'she' are {{c1::lui}} and {{c2::lei}}.
content.explanation: In grammatical descriptions, you might see 'egli' (he) and 'ella' (she), but in modern spoken Italian, 'lui' and 'lei' are exclusively used.
content.spanish: En italiano, los pronombres de sujeto para 'él' y 'ella' son 'lui' y 'lei'.
content.usage: Examples: Lui è bravo. (He is good.) | Lei è italiana. (She is Italian.)

content.text: In Italian, the subject pronoun for 'we' is {{c1::noi}}, and for 'you all (plural)' is {{c2::voi}}.
content.explanation: Unlike Spanish, Italian 'noi' and 'voi' do not change ending for gender (no 'nosotras' or 'vosotras').
content.spanish: En italiano, los pronombres para 'nosotros' y 'vosotros/ustedes' son 'noi' y 'voi' (invariables en género).
content.usage: Examples: Noi siamo amici. (We are friends.) | Voi siete pronti? (Are you all ready?)

content.text: In Italian, the subject pronoun for 'they' is {{c1::loro}}.
content.explanation: Used for both masculine, feminine, or mixed groups.
content.spanish: En italiano, el pronombre para 'ellos/ellas' es 'loro'.
content.usage: Example: Loro sono stanchi. (They are tired.)

content.text: In Italian, what is the infinitive form of the verb 'to be'? {{c1::essere}}
content.explanation: The term "essere" refers to is one of the two auxiliary verbs in Italian.. In context: "In Italian, what is the infinitive form of the verb 'to be'? essere" — memorizing this phrase helps you use it naturally in conversation.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el infinitivo del verbo 'ser/estar' en italiano? essere.
content.usage: Term: essere | Example: essere o non essere (to be or not to be)

content.text: Conjugate 'essere' (to be) in the present tense for 'io' (I) and 'tu' (you): io {{c1::sono}}, tu {{c2::sei}}.
content.explanation: Essere present tense conjugations are highly irregular.
content.spanish: Conjuga 'essere' en presente para 'yo' y 'tú': io sono, tu sei.
content.usage: Examples: Io sono felice. (I am happy.) | Tu sei qui. (You are here.)

content.text: Conjugate 'essere' (to be) in the present tense for 'lui/lei' (he/she) and 'noi' (we): lui/lei {{c1::è}}, noi {{c2::siamo}}.
content.explanation: Note the grave accent on 'è' (is). Without the accent, 'e' means 'and'.
content.spanish: Conjuga 'essere' en presente para 'él/ella' y 'nosotros': lui/lei è, noi siamo.
content.usage: Examples: Lui è stanco. (He is tired.) | Noi siamo a casa. (We are at home.)

content.text: Conjugate 'essere' (to be) in the present tense for 'voi' (you all) and 'loro' (they): voi {{c1::siete}}, loro {{c2::sono}}.
content.explanation: Notice that 'sono' is used for both 'io' (I am) and 'loro' (they are). Context tells them apart.
content.spanish: Conjuga 'essere' en presente para 'vosotros' y 'ellos': voi siete, loro sono.
content.usage: Examples: Voi siete italiani? (Are you all Italian?) | Loro sono stanchi. (They are tired.)

content.text: In Italian, the polite/formal pronoun for 'you (singular)' is {{c1::Lei}}.
content.explanation: It is grammatically feminine (same as 'she') and conjugates with the third-person singular verb form (e.g. 'Lei è' = You are formal), but refers to both men and women.
content.spanish: En italiano, el pronombre formal/cortés para 'usted' es 'Lei' (con mayúscula). Usa la tercera persona singular del verbo.
content.usage: Example: Signore, Lei è molto gentile. (Sir, you are very kind.)

content.text: To make a sentence negative in Italian, you place the word {{c1::non}} directly before the verb. Example: I am not Italian = {{c2::Non sono italiano}}.
content.explanation: Very simple negation structure, placed right before the verb.
content.spanish: Para hacer una oración negativa en italiano, se coloca 'non' justo antes del verbo. 'Non sono italiano' = No soy italiano.
content.usage: Example: Lui non è stanco. (He is not tired.)

content.text: To ask a question in Italian, you do not need to change the word order or use auxiliary verbs; you simply raise your {{c1::intonation}} at the end of the sentence.
content.explanation: Word order remains identical to statements. Just change the pitch.
content.spanish: En italiano, no es necesario cambiar el orden de las palabras para hacer preguntas; solo se eleva la entonación al final.
content.usage: Statement: Sei stanco. (You are tired.) | Question: Sei stanco? (Are you tired?)

content.text: In Italian, possessive adjectives usually require a definite article. For example, 'my book' is '{{c1::il mio libro}}' and 'my house' is '{{c2::la mia casa}}'.
content.explanation: mio/mia are masculine/feminine. Unlike Spanish, you must include 'il/la' in front.
content.spanish: En italiano, los adjetivos posesivos suelen requerir un artículo definido. 'il mio libro' = mi libro.
content.usage: Examples: Questo è il mio libro. (This is my book.) | La mia macchina è rossa. (My car is red.)

content.text: In Italian, 'this' is '{{c1::questo}}' (masc) / '{{c1::questa}}' (fem) and 'that' is '{{c2::quello}}' / '{{c2::quella}}'.
content.explanation: These agree in gender and number with the noun they modify.
content.spanish: En italiano, 'este' se dice 'questo/questa' y 'ese/aquel' se dice 'quello/quella'.
content.usage: Examples: questo tavolo (this table) | quella sedia (that chair)

content.text: In Italian, adjectives must agree in gender and number. If 'He is happy' is 'Lui è felice', 'They (masc) are happy' is '{{c1::Loro sono felici}}'.
content.explanation: Adjectives ending in '-e' become '-i' in the plural for both masculine and feminine.
content.spanish: En italiano, los adjetivos concuerdan en género y número. 'Lui è felice' -> 'Loro sono felici' (las palabras terminadas en -e cambian a -i).
content.usage: Example: Le ragazze sono felici. (The girls are happy.)
